City of Woodland
DEC 13

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY                    CITY HALL        WOODLAND, CALIFORNIA 95695        (916) [illegible]
                                                                                                  661-5907

December 10, 1990


Mr. John Rothman
Calif. Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento CA 95804-0807

RE:  REQUEST FOR OPINION - "TAORMINO PROJECT"

Dear Mr. Rothman:

As a follow-up to my request for an opinion of September 4, 1990,
and your letter received October 4, 1990, and our subsequent
telephone conversation, the City has now obtained, through a
consultant, the information you requested pursuant to FPPC
Regulation 18702.3(d).

I am enclosing a copy of the report done by John C. Lynch, which
provides, in detail, all the information requested pursuant to the
regulation.

I would appreciate it if you could provide us with a definitive
opinion whether indicating that Councilmembers Cresione and Timothy
have a conflict, or that they do not and that the opinion can be
relied on for immunity pursuant to Government Code section
83114(b).

Thank you for your cooperation and prompt attention to this matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

RONALD E. MOE
City Attorney

cc:  Com. Dev. Dir.
     City Manager
     Council

Enclosure


City of Trees

LYNCH & ASSOCIATES
real estate appraisals & consultation

21 court street
woodland, CA 95695

bus. 916-666-6626

December 5, 1990

Janet Ruggiero
Community Development Director
City of Woodland

RE: Information and Advise to FPPC
     Taormino Project

The attached information and advise as requested by City
Attorney, Ronald E. Moe, required by the FPPC is enclosed herein
as you have authorized:

Enclosed are maps showing the proximity of the subject project to
the properties of two council members also the magnitude, change
of use, location, and details of the project.  A discussion of
the impacts on the existing neighborhoods closest to the proposed
development, including the effects to the council members neighborhood. The
study indicates there is little impact regarding traffic, noise,
views, air quality, and property values on the nearby neighbor-
hood or council members homes.  No value increase will be real-
ized from the project by either council members homes.

Thank you for the opportunity to provide the above information
and present my opinions on the effects of the proposed development
on the council members homes.

Respectfully submitted,

John C. Lynch
John C. Lynch

JCL:gm

TAORMINO PROJECT

INFORMATION AND OPINION

AS REQUIRED BY

FPPC


BACKGROUND

The City Council has requested more definitive information and
advice through the City Attorney on the proposed Taormino
Development as it relates to the FPPC regulations. The requested
information includes: (1) the location and proximity of the pro-
posed development in relation to the location of two of the coun-
cil members' residential properties; (2) magnitude of the
proposed project; (3) change of land use; (4) advise on the
economic impacts on the two council members' properties; and (5)
the change of character of the neighborhoods adjacent to the ex-
isting city boundary caused by the proposed development.

LOCATION

Figure 1 shows the location and size of lots of the two council
member's residential properties. The lots are 7424 square feet
and approximately 10000 square feet which is considered typical
of the area. Location and distance of the two homes relative to
the proposed development are shown on Figure 2. The council
members' homes are located about .7 mile or approximately 3000
feet from the proposed residential development. The proposed ex-
tension of Coloma Way at C.R. 24A is approximately 1750 feet from
the homes in question. Church or similar facilities are tenta-
tively proposed for future development along the extended Coloma
Way on the east side. It is not anticipated that the traffic on
College or Coloma Way generated by the proposed subdivision will
go by the council members' homes.

Concerning the requested change of land use from prime farmland
to residential, Figure 3 gives the location and size of existing
farm land parcels proposed for the Taormino development. A total
of approximately 164 acres are included in the planned develop-
ment. Figure 4 provides the location, size, and ownership of farm
land parcels around the Taormino development.

PROPOSED RESIDENTIAL DEVELOPMENT

The preliminary development plan for Taormino's "The Woodlands"
is shown on Figure 6. Principal access to the subdivision will be
provided by the extension of College Street. Access will also be
available by the extension of Coloma Way which will primarily
service the other than residential development in the northeast
corner. Landscaped fencing or walls will extend around the peri-
meter of the development. A total of 275 residential lots will be
developed on 139.58 acres. School/church sites, playground, and a
lineal park will be developed on 25 acres in the northeast corner
of the development.

The arrangement of the proposed lots are shown on Figure 6. The
rectangular lots are planned to be approximately 90 feet by 150
feet for a total of about 13,500 square feet which is larger than
the typical subdivision lot in S.E. Woodland. Taking the average
of 3.04 persons per residential unit, there will be approximately
836 persons living in the subdivision. At the rate of two cars
per family there will be 550 cars added to the daily traffic
flow.

By design, most of the interior street traffic in the proposed
subdivision will be directed to College Street. The increased
traffic on Coloma Way will be to the churches, schools,
playgrounds, and linear park. No access is to be provided from
East Street. Figure 7 shows the location of the proposed
development in relation to the existing neighborhoods.
Typical floor plans and elevations of the homes planned for "The
Woodlands" are shown on Figures 8 & 9. The size of the one story,
3 bedroom, 2 1/2 bath home shown is 2133 square feet.

Taormino plans to start development in the fall of 1992. He esti-
mates that the purchase price of the lots will be around $80,000,
and for a typical 2200 square foot home the total price to be
around $234,000.

NEIGHBORHOOD IMPACTS

Traffic:

There will be increased automobile traffic on College Street and
Coloma Way. It is anticipated that most of the traffic will dis-
perse at the Gibson Road intersections. Most of the traffic to
the south and west will exit the subdivision via College and
County Road 25A. Some traffic headed east to the Regional Mall
and vicinity will use the south route which is slightly longer
than the north route but with less traffic.

Noise:

The following information was obtained from the 1988 General
Plan, City of Woodland, regarding traffic noise on Gibson Road
from College to Norden Way, the 60 decible contour (equivalent to
conversation at 6 feet) would extend 89 feet from Gibson Road if
unobstructed by structures. The council members' residences are
over 800 feet from Gibson and should be minimally affected.

Views:

Extended views of adjoining farmland will be shortened or blocked
from the back yards of those homes from Coloma Way to State Route
113 (frontage road) backing up to the present city limits line.
Views from the homes in the vicinity of the two council members
will not be affected. Nor should a change in privacy be a consid-
eration.

Air Quality:

According to the 1988 General Plan, City of Woodland: Air quality
in the Woodland area is typical of the lower Sacramento Valley
without the high polution levels present in the Sacramento
Metropolitan Area. The most severe existing air quality problem
is the photo chemical oxidant (ozone) which exceeds the standard
30 days a year. Although the degradation of air quality in the
Sacramento Metropolitan Area is primarily related to vehicle usage
the small amount of traffic generated by the Taormino development
would have little effect on the immediate neighborhood or the
City of Woodland.

Values:

Experience has shown that there will be little impact on the
value of council members' residential properties caused by the
new subdivision. A direct relationship exists between the value
of real property and population growth. Because land is fixed
and land cannot be increased in response to change in demand, it
therefore derives its value from the effective demand.

Scarcity of land of a particular type in demand naturally has a
higher value. The demand for land is a direct outgrowth of
population. Consequently, increases in land value reflect
population growth.

The overall growth rate for the years 1975 through 1984 is 2.79
percent. The city has used the growth projections prepared by
the Sacramento Area Council of Governments which are 2.06 percent
per year.

Neither of the council members homes should increase in value due
to the proposed development. Overexpansion in the building in-
dustry is directly reflected by an excess supply of improved
property and a consequent decline in property values. Also a
period of curtailed building activity is directly reflected by a
shortage and consequent increase in demand which in turn is
reflected by higher property values.

The homes in the proposed development will be 20-30 percent
higher in value than the council members' homes (including the
adjacent neighborhood) and a different market or buyer is
indicated. In summary, no increase in value can be ambigated,
only a slight decrease due to increased supply. Impacts on the
nearby neighborhoods should be negligible.

Beneficial Impacts:

The planned schools, churches, playground, and lineal park will
be of service to the neighborhood as well as to the proposed sub-
division.
